specification.topic-statement: The signal handling subsystem intercepts terminal resize and process termination signals via atomic flags, deferring all consequential work to safe points in the event loop.

scope.in-scope: The three handled signals, the self-pipe-style atomic flag mechanism, the resize handling path (dimension query and redraw), the quit handling path (provider shutdown and loop exit), and event-loop integration.
scope.boundary: The structure of the select-based event loop and how it processes other I/O events is covered by the REPL event loop specification.
How providers terminate in-flight HTTP connections is handled by the provider subsystem and is out of scope here.

behavior.signal-disposition:
Three signals are intercepted:
- **Terminal resize (SIGWINCH)**: signals that the terminal dimensions have changed. - **User interrupt (SIGINT)**: signals that the user pressed Ctrl+C at the terminal. - **Termination (SIGTERM)**: signals that an external process has requested shutdown.
All three use the same mechanism: the signal handler is a minimal function that sets an atomic flag and returns immediately. No complex work is performed inside the handler.
behavior.atomic-flag-mechanism:
Each signal has a dedicated `volatile sig_atomic_t` flag. Setting this type is guaranteed to be atomic on the target architecture, making it safe to write from a signal handler and read from the main thread without locks.
The flags are only written by signal handlers; they are read and cleared exclusively by the event loop at designated check points. This separation ensures that all consequential state changes occur in the event loop context, where it is safe to call arbitrary functions.
behavior.resize-handling:
At the start of each event loop iteration, the resize flag is checked. If set, the flag is cleared and the terminal's current dimensions are queried from the kernel. The cached terminal dimensions are updated, and a full screen redraw is performed to reflow the display at the new width.
Terminals may deliver SIGWINCH repeatedly as the user drags the window. Each delivery sets the flag, but the event loop coalesces rapid deliveries into a single redraw because the flag is level-triggered (set-then-cleared), not edge-triggered.
behavior.quit-handling:
Near the end of each event loop iteration, the quit flag is checked. If set (by either SIGINT or SIGTERM), the flag is cleared and the shutdown sequence runs:
1. All active agents are iterated. 2. For each agent, the provider instance (if initialized) is invalidated, which terminates any in-flight HTTP connections. 3. The event loop's exit condition is set, causing the loop to terminate after the current iteration completes.
The quit flag is also set by the action dispatcher when the user presses Ctrl+C while the REPL has keyboard focus. Both paths share the same flag and the same shutdown sequence.
behavior.test-instrumentation: Signal handler functions are excluded from code coverage instrumentation. OS signal delivery cannot be reliably triggered in unit tests, so coverage of these handlers is accepted as a structural gap.
